words: HARRY SAMPSON: I saw Wild Davey. I was nine years old when I came here
SAM SCHRAGER: What do you remember about Wild Davey?

speaker: HS
words: Well, all that I remember is that as kids we were scared of him because he

speaker: HS
words: was kind of a recluse, I guess, you'd call him. We more or less considered

speaker: HS
words: him a hermit, sort of. And he had these dogs which we were afraid of too.

speaker: HS
words: So that he was an individual. He was eccentric. People in town here kiew of

speaker: HS
words: him, so he was, as I say, a character.

speaker: SS
words: Did you see him mostly going by on the street? Is that where you'd see him?

speaker: HS
words: Yeah, we'd see him d3h here. He lived right down here on White Avenue, in

speaker: HS
words: kind of a shack down there. And by the way, too, at that same time we had a

speaker: HS
words: negro family that lived right down in there. that's the first negro family

speaker: HS
words: that I know of that came to Moscow. And they were here before 1902, I'm sure.

speaker: HS
words: Clarice knows the name of the negro family because they were dark negroes.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know how he made a living?

speaker: HS
words: No, I dont. No, I don't.

speaker: SS
words: Did their kids go to school here?

speaker: HS
words: Oh yes, yes. Idon't know how many youngsters they had. I suppose she's

speaker: HS
words: taping there or I'd go up and ask her now. I can't remember; it wasn't a

speaker: HS
words: big negro family at all.

speaker: SS
words: We'll ask her later. Do you now if they stayed for very long?

speaker: HS
words: Well, as far as I know, I don't rmember tham ever leaving.

speaker: HS
words: buried he'e in the cemetery, as far as I know.

speaker: SS
words: This Wild Davey, did he have any friends in Moscow?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, Mr. Creighton was a^ Kooa friend of his. And I wouldn't be

speaker: HS
words: surprised that he saw that he had clothing to wear. And as Clarice

speaker: HS
words: mentioned the last time you were here that the hotel gave him food. And

speaker: HS
words: at that time the hotel was run by Mr. Gale whose daughter married

speaker: HS
words: Mrs. was a Gale.

speaker: SS
words: Do you have any idea why Mr. Creighton and he were friends?

speaker: HS
words: No, I don't.

speaker: SS
words: He just took an interest in the man?

speaker: HS
words: Yeah, evidently did. Creighton was a man that had very few friends, but he

speaker: HS
words: had special friends that he liked he was interested in.'Cause he was an

speaker: HS
words: immigrant; he was a boy from Scotland, came to the United 8tates. And in

speaker: HS
words: fact, as I understood it, his folks wanted to make a sinister out of him,

speaker: HS
words: and he didn't want to, and he over to America. And I don't know how

speaker: HS
words: old he was when he came here and how he happened to come to Moscow, I don't

speaker: HS
words: know. But I worked for Mr. Creighton from 1907 to 1910 I was going to school.

speaker: HS
words: And why I know he had special friends, he would send in to a liquor company

speaker: HS
words: and get very high grade wines and liquors and get oh, a barrel full of the

speaker: HS
words: stuff. And he had a little hideaway in the back of the store where like

speaker: HS
words: someone that he wanted to talk to secretively, and he had that barrel

speaker: HS
words: there. And before Christmas he would make up the packages of these different

speaker: HS
words: people that he was going to give those presents to. And I'm sure Wild

speaker: HS
words: Davey probably, was maybe one of them too. I don't know. But I know there was

speaker: HS
words: a banker in town; he was a special friend. And 4nm ones.

speaker: HS
words: I delivered some of the packages for him.

speaker: SS
words: So really he was the kind of man who didn't hob-nob with all the businessmen

speaker: SS
words: but that kept mostly certain people as friends.

speaker: HS
words: Yeah, he had

speaker: SS
words: Can you tell me—I was thinking about how he is different from Mr. David in

speaker: SS
words: their ways of looking at business and at life. How were they different, those

speaker: SS
words: two men?

speaker: HS
words: Well, Mr. David was a community spirited man. He was interested in the education,

speaker: HS
words: the spiritual life of the town, politically, in fact he could have been

speaker: HS
words: governor of the state if he'd wanted to run for it but he turned it down.

speaker: HS
words: I don't know the reason for it except that it took him away from his business

speaker: HS
words: or something. But Creighton, he and his wife, he had a wife that was very

speaker: HS
words: eccentric. And she only had a very, very few friends too. So they were that

speaker: HS
words: type of people.

speaker: SS
words: Would you say that the two men had a very different philosophy of business?

speaker: HS
words: Well, they got to be very competitive in years on. And I think that was one

speaker: HS
words: of the reasons probably that Mr.  didn't want me to go down to

speaker: HS
words: David's because when I left there he never spoke to me for several years after

speaker: HS
words: I'd been down there. Even his wife wouldn't speak to me.

speaker: SS
words: Would you tell me about what that offer was? You said that he made you an offer

speaker: SS
words: when you came back to say that you were offered a job.

speaker: HS
words: Well, I was I think it was, twenty-fvie dollars a month. And

speaker: HS
words: the opening down there was due to a young fellow that was living at

speaker: HS
words: my mother's place. He and another fellow had a disagreement and they both

speaker: HS
words: quit, so they needed somebody quick. So this fellow told me, he says,"If you

speaker: HS
words: want the job,go down there and see Mr. David." And they were particularly

speaker: HS
words: interested in someone that could understand the Scandinavian people because

speaker: HS
words: this county was probably sixty per cent Scandinavian. So I went down there,

speaker: HS
words: and I just got the job that quick. So I went back and told Creighton about it

speaker: HS
words: and at first he just kinda blew up and wouldn't say anything. And I gave him

speaker: HS
words: plenty of time before I went down to David's. So around and

speaker: HS
words: he says,"Will you stay if I boost your salary?" Well, he boosted up to, I

speaker: HS
words: think it was thirty-five or forty dollars a month. And I had to tell I said, "My offer

speaker: HS
words: down there is better than that, and Ianad already accepted

speaker: HS
words: the job so I couldn't refuse it. I was of that nature. At the same time

speaker: HS
words: I Ml an offer from who run a bookstore, and he offered me the

speaker: HS
words: same amount but I decided to go to David's

speaker: SS
words: I wanted to ask you about your mother's boarding house too. About how that

speaker: SS
words: worked and what it was like.

speaker: HS
words: Well, after my father passaci away, in 1907, I think it was. He left her insurance,

speaker: HS
words: and she was advised to use that insurance to buy a home, she'd have a place

speaker: HS
words: because we were renting at that time. We had sold the home that we had built in

speaker: HS
words: 1902. So that was for sale and we bought it back. There were four of us

speaker: HS
words: youngsters, and I was the oldest at the time, in order to keep the family

speaker: HS
words: together, why she decided to run a boarding house for students, which she did.

speaker: HS
words: And in order to do it,we had three bedroomAupstairs and one down and a parlor.

speaker: HS
words: And she turned the parlor into a bedroom, so that gave her four rooms for

speaker: HS
words: students. Well, she had about six to eight students, they slept in

speaker: HS
words: room. And she was a wonderful And she'd board, and roomed them for

speaker: HS
words: twenty dollars month. And she set a very good table; they all liked her

speaker: HS
words: food. Well, that left it pretty tough for us for sleeping quarters. So

speaker: HS
words: my brother and I, we slept in the dining room in a pull-down bed which we

speaker: HS
words: had to put up every morning so they'd have enough room to eat around a big

speaker: HS
words: dining room table. Then as far as the toilet , of course we had an

speaker: HS
words: outhouse. That was the only thing we had there; the old outhouse with a

speaker: HS
words: crescent over the door and a Sears and Roebuck catalogue.which, had to be

speaker: HS
words: moved about every year or so. water supply, we had to drill our own

speaker: SS
words: Did Mr. Creighton ever mention to you about his getting shot by

speaker: SS
words: Steffens?

speaker: HS
words: Not that I knew of, but I knew of the incident. Because we came here

speaker: HS
words: the spring after all this happened. So it was,I knew that Creighton was

speaker: HS
words: walking down the street and Steffens took a shot at him and he was

speaker: HS
words: on Steffen's list. Creighton and Watkins, Dr. Watkins and seems to me

speaker: HS
words: the sheriff.

speaker: SS
words: I think Jolly was on his list and also Held.

speaker: HS
words: August Held was on the list.

speaker: SS
words: What was the talk when you came about what had happened? Did they have

speaker: SS
words: any motivation for Steffens going after Watkins?

speaker: HS
words: I really don't know. He was a little off balanced but he did have a

speaker: HS
words: grudge against Doc Watkins alright. For some reason or another. I don't

speaker: HS
words: know whether it was because of some medical reason or what.

speaker: SS
words: From what you heard, did Steffens have a history of being unbalanced?

speaker: HS
words: Evidently he showed some peculiarity because the,when he went on this

speaker: HS
words: rampage, he went on horseback. And as soon as it was all over, why he

speaker: HS
words: should go back to his mother's home, he was living with his mother, I

speaker: HS
words: don't know. You'd athought he'd a head out someplace. But he didn't.

speaker: HS
words: And then of course, when they surrounded the place and tried to get

speaker: HS
words: him to surrender, and she finally came out of the house and they had

speaker: HS
words: shot holes into the house. We saw holes in the house years afterwards.

speaker: HS
words: After he was shot. So we don't know whether he shot himself or if he was

speaker: HS
words: shot by a bullet shot through the house.But he was killed right there.

speaker: SS
words: Do you have any idea how the mother took it?

speaker: HS
words: Well I suppose like any mother,she wanted to save her son, didn't want

speaker: HS
words: to see him killed. But took a lot of nerve for her to come out the door

speaker: HS
words: and try to get 'em to quit

speaker: SS
words: Was she telling them he was dead?

speaker: HS
words: As far as I know I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: You think she came out to tell them to stop shooting before he died?

speaker: HS
words: I suppose coming out of the house she thought maybe that would stop the

speaker: HS
words: shooting. There were a lot of men out there with guns that they got from

speaker: HS
words: the hardware store and went out there and the whole sheriff set up.

speaker: HS
words: They were in like trenches like in the Paradise Creek out there.For

speaker: HS
words: protection.

speaker: SS
words: Did the men in the posse ever talk about it in the later years?

speaker: HS
words: The papers were full of it. My father was out here when it happened.

speaker: HS
words: We were still back in Wisconsin. We didn't come out here until about

speaker: HS
words: the first of March. And of course, he had papers so that, oh those big

speaker: HS
words: headlines. I imagine there's papers someplace, at the university or

speaker: HS
words: someplace that show all what happened.

speaker: SS
words: They have the Lewiston paper but not the Moscow one. And they have two

speaker: SS
words: accounts. The one that come out right after he was shot the same day

speaker: SS
words: was quite different than the one that came out the next day. A number

speaker: SS
words: of the details were different. Whether he had killed himself or been

speaker: SS
words: killed. What had happened after he shot Watkins and some of the encounters

speaker: SS
words: that he had in Moscow. Whether the mother had, you heard that the mother

speaker: SS
words: probably came out to get them to stop shooting? While he was still

speaker: SS
words: defending himself?

speaker: HS
words: I would take it that was the reason she came out,yes.

speaker: SS
words: They have that two ways too. She came out before or after he was dead.

speaker: SS
words: Did the men talk about it in later years?

speaker: HS
words: Like any incident of that kind, it never dies down. It sometimes gets

speaker: HS
words: magnified as to what all happened. I remember seeing some headline of

speaker: HS
words: what happened, alright,but I've kind of forgot a lot of it.

speaker: SS
words: Its been suggested to me that some of these guys had some doubts in

speaker: SS
words: later years, did you ever hear that any of they guys in the posse said

speaker: SS
words: that they were sorry? That they'd handled it that way?

speaker: HS
words: I don't remember reading anything about that.

speaker: SS
words: I was thinking in the talk.

speaker: HS
words: No, I don't remember anything of that.

speaker: HS
words: well so we had a pump which we had to carry water into the kitchen for drinking

speaker: HS
words: cooking and all that. And bathroom and , having students,

speaker: HS
words: the younger fellows, they had to take a bath in the kitchen in the tub,

speaker: HS
words: a metal tub. Mother would have on the back of the stove alboiler, boiling

speaker: HS
words: hot water. And they'd just take water out of there and put it in the tub

speaker: HS
words: and that'e where they took their bath. Well then, some, we had an electrical

speaker: HS
words: engineering student that stayed there Wallace, and Mother wouldn't let

speaker: HS
words: them use very big light bulbs because the electricty was rather expensive .

speaker: HS
words: So he worked out an idea of where he could put a wire ahead of the meter and

speaker: HS
words: into his room, wich he did. And as far as I know, that operated for

speaker: HS
words: years that way. We didn't know anything about it but we found out afterwards.

speaker: HS
words: These fellows were all around twenty, twenty-one, twenty-two, along in there.

speaker: HS
words: One was a mining engineer, one was en electrical engineer, two of *em were

speaker: HS
words: civil engineers. And it's remarkable, we kept tab of those fellows after

speaker: HS
words: they graduated and where they went, and every one of them made good. I

speaker: HS
words: remember Harry Driscoll, for one, which is family, that

speaker: HS
words: he lived there. And he operated a business out of Troy and made a mint

speaker: HS
words: of money on that. Another fellow was from Genesee, Ole Pauls. He wound up

speaker: HS
words: in the federal government with road construction and bridges and so on.

speaker: SS
words: Did most of these fellows stay for several years in the boarding house?

speaker: HS
words: Oh yes, oh yes. She operated that, oh, for about six or seven years. In

speaker: HS
words: fact, I was working at Creighton's at the time when she started it. And she

speaker: HS
words: carried it on even for sevev£years after I went down to David's.

speaker: SS
words: Did these students go out on dates in those days?

speaker: HS
words: Oh yes, oh you bet.

speaker: SS
words: University women at the time? Is that who they'd.

speaker: HS
words: Yes, yes there were. I could tell you one incident, but it wouldn't do to

speaker: HS
words: record it. One of them fellows that stayed there.

speaker: SS
words: Maybe you could tell it without using his name.

speaker: HS
words: Well, I don't think it would be, kind of an immoral thing.

speaker: SS
words: Where the wood came from that you heated with?

speaker: HS
words: Well, we had a woodstove in the living room and dining room, and

speaker: HS
words: a woodstove—wood and coal range that she cooked on. And we had what they

speaker: HS
words: called, the people that lived out in the mountains, they called *em woodrats.

speaker: HS
words: And they, during the wintertime, they would cut four foot length timber and

speaker: HS
words: sell it then the next summer for the  As I remember my

speaker: HS
words: father used to buy eight cords of wood at a time. And we had a woodshed, piled

speaker: HS
words: it outside the woodshed. And there was a fellow named Stanley who would,

speaker: HS
words: two of the sons are still living in Moscow, Hubert Stanley and Rick Stanley,

speaker: HS
words: he had a saw machine put on the back of the truck and he would cut those into

speaker: HS
words: sixteen or eighteen foot lengths—yeah, sixteen foot lengths 'cause it was

speaker: HS
words: four foot cordwood. And then both  sister and brother pile the wood

speaker: HS
words: in the woodshed. And then a little later on, why we mixed coal along with

speaker: HS
words: the wood to get more heat.

speaker: SS
words: And generally the cost of things was very, very low then.

speaker: HS
words: Oh, as I remember yoid buy a cord of wood for oh, around four and a half

speaker: HS
words: to six dollars a cord in those days.

speaker: SS
words: Your mother could actually make a living by the rates that she was charging

speaker: SS
words: these fellows.

speaker: HS
words: I don't know how she did it, but she was interested in my two sisters taking

speaker: HS
words: music. And she bought a piano on a monthly payment basis and paid that out.

speaker: HS
words: And my two sisters learned to play the piano* As far as my brother and myself

speaker: HS
words: were concerned, at that time there was a mandolin club organized in Moscow,

speaker: HS
words: a man from Spokane came down and gave lessons. And the instructor, it was a

speaker: HS
words: guitar fiayer, and was a man named Childers who run a ice cream

speaker: HS
words: parlor, played a guitar. And then I played the guitar so there were three of

speaker: HS
words: us and about oh, there must have been about twenty-five mandolins.

speaker: HS
words: He furnished fem a mandlin for nothing and give 'em instructions. And

speaker: HS
words: I think the course, for six lessons, was twelve dollars. When we finished

speaker: HS
words: the course, why we gave the concert injthe Mthodist church for the city.

speaker: SS
words: And the people could play it after six lessons then?

speaker: HS
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Pretty good.

speaker: HS
words: Yeah. And after that was through, why then there were four us, formed a

speaker: HS
words: little mandolin club of our own and played at churches and lodges and

speaker: HS
words: different organizations around town. And that group was—I went down yesterday

speaker: HS
words: to see the Eggan girl, who her cousin was one of the group. There was

speaker: HS
words: Eggan, Jenny Peterson, it was Claus Peterson's daughter, and my sister, Myrtle,

speaker: HS
words: and myself. And we played for, oh, a good many years after that. Until,

speaker: HS
words: really our final playing was up at the Lutheran church, which I was a member

speaker: HS
words: of. And oh, our pieces were waltzes and two-steps and things like that.

speaker: HS
words: We didn't play any religious hymns or anything like that. And we had enough

speaker: HS
words: Fundamentalists in the church that objected to having that kind of muslt

speaker: HS
words: in the church. We never played after that.

speaker: SS
words: You weren't playing at achurch service, just in the church, 8r were you

speaker: SS
words: playing it for a service?

speaker: HS
words: It was in evening service of some kind, it wasn't a church service but

speaker: HS
words: it was in the church. And they didn't think that was the place for that

speaker: HS
words: kind of music. But ' the days when we had a lot cff people in

speaker: HS
words: Moscow that were against playing cards and dancing, both. A lot of them

speaker: HS
words: were Methodists. I think the Presbyterians were a little more lenient.

speaker: HS
words: Lutherans were against it too.

speaker: HS
words: SAM I've been told that it was really those people who felt strongly that way

speaker: HS
words: that did bring about prohibition coming to Moscow when it came.

speaker: HS
words: That's right, that's right. And we had prohibition pretty early. It

speaker: HS
words: seems to me it was before World War I.

speaker: SS
words: I think it was.

speaker: SS
words: A lot of temperance lectures or it was put on the ballot or what happened?

speaker: HS
words: It was on the ballot of course. The WCTU ^ere the instigators of the

speaker: HS
words: whole thing. And they passed around a lot of propaganda. And of course

speaker: HS
words: during prohibition the little towns across the line, Uniontown and Cotton,

speaker: HS
words: and those places were still open. Washington didn't have prohibition.

speaker: HS
words: Of course, it was very easy to get liquor and just over there. It

speaker: HS
words: got to the point where the shertfiswould set up a posse out here on the

speaker: HS
words: south Main Street and Bridge and check cars that they thought were coming

speaker: HS
words: over from Uniontown with liquor. And then when it got more severe

speaker: HS
words: the bootleggers came in here and they went around and took individual orders,

speaker: HS
words: never delivered right on the spot. They took orders and then it was delivered

speaker: HS
words: later. I remember.incidences like that.

speaker: SS
words: When you say it got more severe, do you mean it was harder to get, and you

speaker: SS
words: just couldn't go across into Washington and get it.

speaker: HS
words: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: SS
words: So was it openly violated? I mean it was done very quietly, but still it

speaker: SS
words: was pretty broadly disregarded.

speaker: HS
words: Yeah, that was it. Because they knew that they were watching the liquor

speaker: HS
words: coming in. But it got so: common that they couldn't enforce it very much.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard it said that it brought about more drinking because it was illegal

speaker: SS
words: That more people got interested It, it was forbidden.

speaker: HS
words: That's why prohibition was overturned, just for that reason. That there

speaker: HS
words: was more drinking. And a lot of people were gettin liquor from stills

speaker: HS
words: out in the mountains. And peojie were operating stills whth were

speaker: HS
words: well, there were cases where people went blind drinking some of the liquor.

speaker: HS
words: It wasn't properly distilled. And so that's what defeated prohibition

speaker: HS
words: when Roosevelt came in. I've forgotten what year he must have been in

speaker: HS
words: the early thirties.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I wanted to ask you a little bit more about Mr. David, but first

speaker: SS
words: I was going to ask you about that book that you mentioned to me, that

speaker: SS
words: was an influence on you in your early years.

speaker: HS
words: Yeah. Well, Mr. David, senior, Frank David, took quite an interest in me,

speaker: HS
words: and I was interested in my job. I did a lot of things that an ordinary fellow

speaker: HS
words: wouldn't do. And he appreciated me doing more than I was expected. And I

speaker: HS
words: quite an interest in selling and he realised that 'cause I would

speaker: HS
words: tell him when I made a good sale and things like that. So he had a book,

speaker: HS
words: really a book on psychology, 'cause he said selling is all psychology anyway.

speaker: HS
words: And in this book, he took it rather seriously too, himself. He said, "You

speaker: HS
words: know, you can tell a customer by his handshake, whether it's a limp shake

speaker: HS
words: or a real hearty shake. And you can also judge a customer by the coarseness

speaker: HS
words: of his hairl' Things of that type.

speaker: SS
words: What did coarse hair mean?

speaker: HS
words: Well, and I found this particularly true with a lot of customers. A person

speaker: HS
words: with coarse black hair normally bought coarse materials  clothing, like

speaker: HS
words: tweeds and things like that. It wasn't for them to buy because it

speaker: HS
words: accentuated their hair.

speaker: SS
words: But they were attracted to it because it was like their hair?

speaker: HS
words: Evidently.

speaker: HS
words: hat would a limp handshake or astrong handshake indicate?

speaker: SS
words: What was a handshake?wnat

speaker: HS
words: Well, a very weak person, a strong person would be harder to wait on than

speaker: HS
words: the other. The weaker were more, easily influenced in to what you were trying

speaker: HS
words: to sell them. Acid even their walk and things like that, all those things are

speaker: HS
words: in to selling.

speaker: HS
words: This was a book about selling? Or a book about psychology, or both?

speaker: HS
words: That was the Psychology of Selling is what it was.

speaker: HS
words: Did you find that book useful?

speaker: HS
words: Yes, oh yes. I reft that thing through and through, time and time. And

speaker: HS
words: then up at the university they gave a night course, I'd the business

speaker: HS
words: school and the psycfelogy department and I took that in for about three months,

speaker: HS
words: I guess. And then we had a fellow come thiRigh here, most of the

speaker: HS
words: business people on Main Street attended it. What is the fellow that national

speaker: HS
words: advertised?

speaker: SS
words: Is that Dale Carnegie?

speaker: HS
words: Yeah, Dale Carnegie. That's it. And they sent a man in here and he set up

speaker: HS
words: a week's course sellling. And I think that was brought about by the^school

speaker: HS
words: at the university which was very helpful.

speaker: SS
words: Did you seek advice from Mr. David very often the te chnique and the

speaker: SS
words: skills involved in the work. Did you find him helpful in.

speaker: HS
words: Very much so because he was a salesman himself. In fact he was a good

speaker: HS
words: salesman. And he would come around and talk to me about selling. And naturally

speaker: HS
words: I would listen to him and probably ask questions from him about the technique

speaker: HS
words: of selling.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have very much opportunity to watch him working in action?

speaker: HS
words: Well, Mr. David, at the time I went inthere was more of a

speaker: HS
words: floor walker. He did very little selling, but he'g direct customers to

speaker: HS
words: people he'd want to wait on 'em. And he was a great storyteller.

speaker: HS
